heading: Punctuation Clues
body: Punctuation can show how English groups a compound sentence. That helps identify the **main operator**, the connector that governs the whole sentence. This builds on the earlier idea of the main operator.

examples.text: "If Alice goes, and Bob goes, then Charlie stays" has visible grouping marks.

heading: Commas Can Mislead
body: A comma does not name the main operator by itself. It may only mark a pause inside a larger structure, especially inside a standard conditional.

examples.text: In "If Alice goes, and Bob goes, then Charlie stays," the main operator is conjunction.
In "If Alice goes, and Bob goes, then Charlie stays," the main operator is conditional.

heading: If Then Outside
body: When a sentence starts with "if" and reaches a matching "then," the whole sentence is usually a **conditional**, even if commas mark smaller parts inside it.

examples.text: "If the light is on, and the door is shut, then the room is ready" is governed by $\rightarrow$.

note: The antecedent can contain its own conjunction or disjunction.

heading: Semicolons Split Blocks
body: A **semicolon** often separates larger clause blocks. First read the block on each side, then ask which connector joins those two blocks. That connector is often the main operator.

examples.text: "Alice packs, and Bob cooks; or Charlie orders takeout" has disjunction as the main operator.
"Alice packs, and Bob cooks; or Charlie orders takeout" has conjunction as the main operator.

heading: Worked Cases
body: Read grouped parts first. Then step outward until the whole sentence is covered by one operator.

examples.text: "If Alice goes, and Bob goes, then Charlie stays" has main operator conditional.
"Alice goes, and Bob goes, or Charlie stays" has main operator disjunction.
"Alice goes; and Bob stays" has main operator conjunction.

note: Use punctuation as a clue, not as a substitute for reading the whole sentence.

heading: Read Outward
body: Find the smallest grouped pieces first. Then move outward to the connector that links the largest parts. The outermost connector is the main operator.

examples.text: In "If Alice goes, and Bob goes, then Charlie stays," first see the inner conjunction, then the outer conditional.

heading: Core Method
body: Use commas and semicolons to spot grouping. Then identify the connector that governs the whole sentence, not the connector inside a smaller part.
